She'd carried them for six years, through a dozen different ships and twice as many jobs, and she'd never played with anyone who mattered. "Do you know games?" she asked. Echo's head tilted. Her expression didn't change, but something in her posture shifted—curiosity, maybe. Or just attention. "It's okay if you don't. I can teach you." Beans shuffled the cards, her fingers moving automatically. "It's called Bastard. Stupid name, I know. My sisters taught me. We used to play after shifts, when we were supposed to be sleeping." She dealt out two hands. "You don't have to talk. You don't have to do anything. I just—I thought maybe you'd want—" She stopped. Her voice had cracked, and she couldn't figure out why. It was just a card game. It didn't mean anything. Echo reached out and picked up her hand. She didn't smile. She didn't speak. But she looked at the cards, and then at Beans, and then she arranged them in her fingers with a precision that suggested she'd understood the game before the first card was dealt. "Okay," Beans said, and her voice was steady now. "Okay. I'll start." They played until the ship's lighting shifted to day-cycle, and Beans finally went to her bunk and slept. --- On the fourteenth day, Dax found Mara in the cargo hold. She was standing over Echo's crate again—the one they'd never sealed back up, the one with the broken lock and the scraped-off symbols. She'd been coming here more often, and she wasn't sure why. Maybe because it was the only part of the ship that was still. "Arm's not getting better," Dax said, leaning against the doorframe. He looked older than he had two weeks ago—greyer, more tired. "Nerve damage. I can feel my hand about half the time, and the other half it's like it belongs to someone else." "Can you still work?" "Enough. I've had worse." He moved to stand beside her, looking down at the crate. "She's a strange one. I've been running scans when she lets me. Her cellular structure is regenerating, but slowly. Whatever she did in the belt, it took something out of her. I don't know if she'll ever get it back." "Is she dying?" "Not dying. Just... diminished. Like a battery that won't hold a full charge." He paused. "She doesn't seem to mind. Or if she minds, she doesn't show it. Hard to tell with her." Mara nodded. She'd noticed the same thing—the way Echo moved more slowly now, the faint tremor in her hands, the long periods of stillness that looked like exhaustion or meditation or something in between. "The sanctuary," she said. "You think they can help her?" "I think they can try. I don't know if help is what she needs." Dax shifted his weight, favoring his bad arm. "She's not like us, Mara. She's not broken in ways that can be fixed. She's just—different. And maybe the best thing we can do is stop trying to make her fit into categories she was never meant for." "What categories?" "Person. Property. Tool. Weapon." He shook his head. "She's all of those things and none of them. And I've spent forty years thinking I understood how bodies work, how minds work, and she's shown me more in two weeks than I learned in a lifetime." He looked at Mara. "I don't know what she is. But I know she's worth protecting. Even if I don't understand why." Mara didn't respond. After a while, Dax left. After a while longer, so did she. --- The sanctuary was a station called Horizon. It appeared on sensors three days before they arrived—a cluster of modules and habitats on the edge of a nebula, running without transponders, unmarked on any official charts. The kind of place where people went when they needed to stop being found. Mara hailed them on an encrypted frequency. A woman's voice came back, older and roughened by years of recycled air: "You're expected. Dock at module seven. Someone will meet you." No questions. No demands. Just acceptance. Mara stood on the bridge and watched the station grow larger in the viewscreen. Behind her, the crew had gathered—Kade by the door, Beans at the auxiliary console, Dax in the corridor with his bad arm in a sling. And Echo, standing at the back of the group, wrapped in her blanket, her dark eyes fixed on the station ahead. "We don't have to stay," Mara said. "We drop her off, we move on. No one's asking us to—" "She doesn't want to stay." Everyone turned. Beans was staring at Echo, her face pale. "She showed me. Just now. When she knew we were getting close." Beans shook her head. "She doesn't want to stay here with strangers. She wants to—" The pilot stopped, struggling. "She wants to stay with us. If we'll have her." Mara looked at Echo. The girl's expression hadn't changed. But there was something in her eyes now—something that looked almost like hope. "You don't know us," Mara said. "You don't know what we are. What we've done." Echo's hand came up. She pressed her palm against her own chest, over her heart, and then extended it toward Mara. *We're the same.* Mara heard it. Not with her ears—with something else, something Echo had opened in her back on Thetys. A connection that hadn't fully closed. *We're the same. We're all trying to stop being what we were made into.* She didn't respond. She couldn't. There was a tightness in her chest that she hadn't felt in years. "Captain," Kade said quietly. "What do you want to do?" Mara looked at her crew. Kade, with his violence and his loyalty and his desperate need to be more than a weapon. Dax, with his failed career and his soft heart and his refusal to stop fixing what was broken. Beans, with her fear and her counting and her worn deck of cards. And Echo, who had been made in a laboratory and had somehow become a person anyway. She thought about Thetys. About the door she hadn't opened. About the choice she'd made to stand and do nothing while people died. And she thought about the choice she was making now. "Set a course," she said. "Away from here. Somewhere no one's looking." "Captain—" Beans started. "We're not dropping her off." Mara turned to face the viewscreen, watching the station slide past as Beans adjusted their trajectory. "She stays. Until she decides otherwise." No one argued. No one cheered. The decision settled into the ship, into the crew, and became part of the *Ridgerunner's* story. --- The last night before they reached open space, Mara found Echo in the observation blister. The girl was sitting in her usual spot, her knees drawn up, her face tilted toward the stars. The nebula painted her grey skin in shades of violet and blue. She looked older in this light—ancient, almost, like something that had seen the birth and death of suns. Mara sat beside her. She didn't speak. She'd learned, over three weeks, that Echo didn't need words. After a while, Echo's hand moved. Her fingers brushed against Mara's wrist—not her temple this time, just a touch, light and brief. *Thank you.* Mara felt the words settle into her. She didn't respond. She didn't need to. They sat together in the dark, watching the stars wheel past, and the *Ridgerunner* carried them forward into the black. No destination. No certainty. Just a ship, a crew, and a creature who had been made in a laboratory and had somehow chosen to be something more. Mara didn't know what came next. She didn't know if they'd find safety, or if Corso's employers would catch up with them, or if Echo's abilities would fade entirely. She didn't know if she'd ever stop hearing the banging on that sealed door, or if the weight of thirty-seven dead would ever stop pressing against her chest. But she knew one thing. She'd opened a door. Finally, after eleven years, she'd opened a door. That was something. That was a start. The ship hummed around her, and the stars stretched out ahead, and somewhere in the distance, something like a future was waiting to be found.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter serves as a contemplative denouement to what appears to be an action-heavy space western narrative. The writing demonstrates considerable technical competence and emotional maturity, though it suffers from some structural and pacing issues that prevent it from achieving its full potential. The character work is the chapter's strongest element. Each crew member receives meaningful development that feels organic to their established personalities. Kade's confession about his past violence and his struggle with identity is particularly well-handled - the dialogue feels authentic to his character profile, and his admission that he's "someone who's good at violence and trying to be less bad at everything else" captures his internal conflict without melodrama. Mara's parallel revelation about Thetys creates a satisfying thematic resonance, showing how both characters are grappling with past moral failures. The relationship between Beans and Echo through the card game is genuinely touching and avoids sentimentality. The author wisely shows rather than tells this connection, using the simple act of teaching a game to convey deeper themes about belonging and communication. Echo's characterization remains consistent with her profile as something "other" - neither fully person nor property - and her telepathic communications feel appropriately alien without being overwrought. However, the chapter suffers from significant pacing problems. The three-week time jump creates a sense of narrative drift that, while thematically appropriate, makes the chapter feel somewhat inert. The structure relies heavily on vignettes and character moments without sufficient forward momentum. The decision to have Echo stay with the crew, while emotionally satisfying, feels somewhat inevitable rather than dramatically earned. The prose style is generally strong, maintaining the lean Western aesthetic established in the plan. The author avoids purple prose and forced metaphors, instead relying on concrete details and understated emotion. Lines like "She'd opened a door. Finally, after eleven years, she'd opened a door" work effectively as both literal and metaphorical statements without feeling heavy-handed. The dialogue remains true to each character's established voice patterns. Kade's rural inflections, Mara's clipped authority, and Beans's nervous energy all come through clearly. The conversations feel natural rather than expository, particularly the exchange between Mara and Kade on the bridge. The chapter follows its outlined plan faithfully, hitting all the major beats: Dax's injury, the crew's psychological aftermath, the card game between Beans and Echo, and the final decision at Horizon station. The ending's ambiguity aligns well with the stated intention to avoid neat resolutions. Some weaknesses emerge in the execution. The medical details about Dax's injury feel slightly clinical and detached. The Horizon station sequence, while serving the plot, lacks the specificity and atmosphere that characterizes the stronger sections. The final metaphor about opening doors, while thematically appropriate, borders on being too neat for a story that otherwise resists easy symbolism. The chapter succeeds in creating genuine emotional investment in these characters and their relationships. The found family dynamic feels earned rather than imposed, and the decision to keep Echo aboard resonates because it grows naturally from the established character dynamics rather than being forced by plot necessity. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious space western that attempts to blend hard sci-fi elements with character-driven drama. The story follows Captain Mara Vance and her crew aboard the *Ridgerunner* as they discover their cargo is actually a sentient being named Echo, leading to a moral crisis about whether to complete their delivery contract. **Strengths:** The world-building is solid and lived-in. Details like Keeler Station's "ozone and fried protein" smell, the *Ridgerunner's* patched systems, and the economic desperation driving the crew create a believable universe. The space western genre is well-executed with appropriate technology levels and frontier atmosphere. Character development shows genuine effort. Mara's backstory involving the Thetys incident provides meaningful motivation, and each crew member has distinct personality traits and speech patterns. Echo as a mysterious, telepathic being is handled with restraint - her abilities are shown rather than explained at length. The plot maintains good momentum across eight chapters, with escalating stakes and meaningful choices. The pursuit by Corso creates genuine tension, and his eventual change of heart feels earned through his interactions with Echo. **Weaknesses:** The dialogue often feels stilted and overly functional. Characters frequently speak in exposition-heavy chunks that don't sound natural: "She's not cargo. She's a survivor. Someone killed all the others, and she got away, or they let her go, or—" This tell-don't-show approach weakens emotional moments. Some character transformations feel rushed. Corso's complete reversal from determined pursuer to protector happens too quickly, despite the telepathic revelation. Similarly, Kade's philosophical awakening about violence feels underdeveloped. The prose occasionally veers into purple territory with forced metaphors: "the ship's systems struggling to maintain the bare essentials of flight" and "the weight of thirty-seven dead would ever stop pressing against her chest." These moments feel overwrought rather than poetic. The ending, while thematically appropriate, lacks concrete resolution. The crew's decision to keep Echo feels more like avoiding the central conflict than resolving it. The sanctuary subplot is introduced and abandoned without clear purpose. **Technical Issues:** Pacing suffers in the middle chapters where action sequences (the asteroid belt chase) feel rushed while character moments drag. The telepathic visions are inconsistently described - sometimes vivid and specific, other times vague. Some plot elements strain credibility. The crew's immediate acceptance of Echo's humanity despite her alien nature feels too easy, and the corporate conspiracy behind her creation remains frustratingly vague. **Overall Assessment:** This is competent genre fiction with genuine ambition. The author demonstrates understanding of space opera conventions and creates a morally complex scenario. However, execution issues with dialogue, pacing, and character development prevent it from reaching its potential. The story succeeds as adventure fiction but falls short of the deeper emotional resonance it attempts. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 12 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 11 Amateurish: 10 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 62.9/100
+ Possession Horror — Different Eyes (Chapter Avg: 82.9 | Final: 56.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I&#x27;d been sleeping. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Character Possibilities **The Narrator:** - A husband watching his wife become something unrecognizable - A child watching their mother—innocence meeting incomprehensible horror - An elderly person being cared for by their possessed daughter/caregiver - A recovering addict who fears no one will believe them - Someone bedbound or disabled, trapped with a possessed caregiver - The possessING entity, unaware of their own nature (unreliable narrator twist) - A psychiatric patient whose claims of possession are dismissed as delusion **"She" (The Watcher):** - A loving wife now hosting something ancient and hungry - A mother whose possession manifests in twisted "protection" of her child - A girlfriend who dabbled in something she shouldn't have - A woman who was possessed long ago—the entity simply "waking up" now - A willing vessel who made a deal, now regretting it - A victim of generational possession passed through bloodline - Someone possessed by a deceased loved one refusing to let go ## Plot Directions **Traditional Possession Arc:** Man discovers wife is possessed, watches her deterioration, attempts to save her. Classic but perhaps too familiar. **The Slow Replacement:** The entity has been there for years, slowly erasing the original personality. The staring is because the entity is now confident enough to stop pretending—studying its final obstacle (the narrator). **Reversed Perspective:** The narrator is the possessing entity. "She" is the original soul, trapped in her own body, watching the intruder. The story slowly reveals this horrible truth. **The Voluntary Vessel:** She invited something in—desperation, grief, a deal made in darkness. Now the narrator must confront what she's become and why she made that choice. **Generational Horror:** Possession runs in her bloodline. Her mother had it, her grandmother before. The narrator discovers he married into a curse, and the staring is the entity deciding if he's worthy of... continuation. **The Child in Peril:** A father notices his wife watching their daughter sleep. The entity wants a younger, fresher host. The ticking clock of potential transfer. **The Shared Possession:** The entity can jump between them. Neither knows who is currently "themselves." Paranoia as the foundation of domestic horror. **Institutional Setting:** Narrator is a patient in a facility; "she" is a nurse possessed by something that feeds on suffering. ## Tone Options - Slow-burn domestic dread (Hereditary meets The Exorcist) - Visceral body horror with graphic transformation - Psychological ambiguity—is she possessed or mentally ill? - Tragic romance horror (love vs. survival) - Cosmic/eldritch horror (the entity is incomprehensible) - Folk horror (tied to specific cultural beliefs/curses) ## Story Beats Across 8 Chapters **Possible Structure A (Discovery Arc):** 1. First incident of staring, establish relationship 2. More incidents, rationalize them 3. Physical/behavioral changes begin 4. First confrontation, denial 5. Discovery of cause (object, place, person) 6. Escalation, violence, revelation of entity's nature 7. Attempt at solution (exorcism, ritual, etc.) 8. Climax and resolution **Possible Structure B (Already Gone):** 1. The staring—something is already wrong 2. Flashbacks to "before" contrast with now 3. The entity begins speaking through her 4. Discovery that she's been possessed for months/years 5. The entity's agenda revealed 6. The original "she" fights back from inside 7. A moment of hope—she breaks through 8. Twist: she was the entity all along, or tragic sacrifice **Possible Structure C (The Investigation):** 1. The staring, first seeds of dread 2. Research phase—he investigates what's happening 3. She catches him researching, tension rises 4. Discovery of the entity's rules/weaknesses 5. First attempt to banish it—fails badly 6. She disappears/transforms fully 7. He must find her/it before the final ritual completes 8. Confrontation at the site of original possession ## Thematic Angles - Identity: Who are we if our bodies can be stolen? - Love: Can you love someone who is no longer themselves? - Trust: The person closest to you is the greatest threat - Parenthood: Would you sacrifice yourself for your child? - Faith: Traditional possession ties to religion; could subvert - Grief: Possession as metaphor for not letting go - Control: Autonomy, consent, violation ## Potential Endings - **Bleak:** She is lost entirely; he must kill her or be killed - **Hopeful:** Exorcism succeeds, but relationship is shattered - **Ambiguous:** She seems saved, but final scene suggests otherwise - **Twist:** He was the target all along; she was protecting him - **Tragic:** She sacrifices herself to save him from the entity - **Cycle:** They defeat this entity, but their child shows signs - **Transference:** The entity jumps to the narrator # Reflection Looking at these options, I need to consider what will be most compelling for 8 chapters of approximately 1000 words each. That's substantial space—enough for real character development and slow-burn dread, but not so much that I can meander. **What works:** The *domestic setting* is powerful. Horror hits hardest when it invades safe spaces—a marriage, a home, a bed. The prompt's intimacy (watching someone while sleeping) suggests the narrator should be close to the possessed person. A husband-wife dynamic gives emotional stakes and allows for rich contrast between "before" and "after." The *slow realization* angle works better than immediate horror. Eight chapters allow me to build dread progressively. Starting with something as simple as staring, then escalating, gives natural pacing. The reader should feel the narrator's denial, then uncertainty, then fear. *First-person narration* from the husband's POV creates immediacy and restriction—we only know what he knows, which limits information in effective ways for horror. His unreliability (denial, fear, love clouding judgment) adds texture. Making the wife a *fully realized character* before possession is crucial. If we care about her, her loss matters. I should show who she was through memories and the fragments that remain. The *folk horror/cultural possession* angle intrigues me more than traditional Catholic exorcism tropes. It could involve something tied to her heritage, a place she visited, or an object. This feels fresher and allows for unique mythology. **What doesn't work:** The *reversed perspective twist* (narrator is the entity) is clever but might feel like a gimmick that overshadows emotional resonance. It could work as a shorter piece but might frustrate over 8 chapters. *High-concept premises* (shared possession, jumping entities) risk becoming too plot-heavy at the expense of character and atmosphere. *A child narrator* would be compelling but limiting—the scope of what they can do and understand constrains the story. *Traditional demon/religious exorcism* feels overdone. The Exorcist looms large, and retreading that ground invites unfavorable comparison. *Mental illness ambiguity* could be powerful but might feel like a cop-out in a possession story. The supernatural element should be real. **What I'm gravitating toward:** A husband watching his wife become possessed by something ancient that she encountered unknowingly. The staring is the first crack in the entity's camouflage—it's studying him, deciding what to do. The progression: staring → small behavioral changes → physical changes → moments of the "real" her breaking through → full manifestation → confrontation → resolution. I want the entity to have *motivation* and *rules*. Not just evil for evil's sake, but something that wants something specific. Perhaps it needs the narrator for something, which is why it hasn't just killed him. This creates tension and a ticking clock. The *ending* needs careful thought. A completely bleak ending might feel hollow after 8 chapters of investment. A completely triumphant ending might feel unearned in horror. I'm drawn to something bittersweet—perhaps she's saved, but damaged; or she sacrifices herself; or the entity is banished but left a mark that suggests it could return. The *cultural/personal connection* to the possession should matter. Perhaps she encountered it at a significant location, or it's tied to her family history, or she unconsciously invited it through grief or trauma. This gives the possession meaning beyond random victimhood. **Questions to resolve:** - What is the entity, specifically? I need clear rules and abilities. - How did she encounter it? The origin matters for the resolution. - Can she be saved? What's the cost? - What does the entity want with the narrator? - How do I balance slow-burn with momentum across 8 chapters?
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Plot:** Michael watches his wife Elena become possessed by an ancient entity she encountered during a trip to her grandmother's village in rural Spain six months ago. The entity has been slowly erasing Elena's personality and is now confident enough to stop pretending—the staring is the entity studying him, testing boundaries, enjoying his fear. The entity feeds on devotion and needs Michael's love to fully anchor itself in this world. Elena is still trapped inside, occasionally breaking through. Michael must discover the truth, research the entity's origins, and attempt a dangerous separation ritual—but the cost will be devastating. **Voice & Style:** First-person present tense from Michael's POV. Intimate, confessional, gradually building dread. Unreliable narrator through denial, not dishonesty. Sensory-focused prose emphasizing domestic detail invaded by wrongness. No gore for shock value—horror emerges from familiarity becoming unrecognizable. **Tone:** Slow-burn domestic dread with psychological depth. Horror is quiet, pervasive, intimate. Romance and loss ground the supernatural. **Aims:** Progressive escalation that earns its terror. Genuine emotional investment in the relationship before dissolution. An entity with clear rules, history, and motivation. A bittersweet resolution that acknowledges cost. **Avoids:** Catholic exorcism tropes, mental illness ambiguity teases, twist endings that undermine emotional investment, violence-as-substitute-for-tension. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – The Watching** Opens with the prompt. Michael pretends to sleep while Elena stares from the bedside. Establishes their loving marriage and recent distance he's attributed to her grief over her grandmother's death. Small domestic moments feel almost-normal but wrong. Ends with him convincing himself it's insomnia, stress—nothing more. **Chapter 2 – Slippage** More staring incidents. Michael catalogues changes: appetite shifts, forgotten habits, her cold hands. She speaks Spanish in her sleep—unusual. He gently confronts; she dismisses him coldly, a flash of something dismissive and cruel in her eyes, then warmth returns. He buries his fear. **Chapter 3 – The First Wound** Elena hurts him accidentally—or not. Her strength is wrong. A disturbing moment: she doesn't recognize their cat, then crushes it with casual indifference. She "wakes up" horrified, the real Elena breaking through briefly, terrified. Michael can no longer deny something is profoundly wrong. **Chapter 4 – The Origin** Michael researches secretly. Finds Elena's travel journal from Spain, hidden. Learns of her grandmother's "sickness"—the same symptoms. A village legend of an entity passed through bloodlines. Elena catches him reading; the entity speaks through her, mocking, revealing it's been inside her for months. It wants him to know. **Chapter 5 – The Terms** The entity reveals its nature and purpose. It feeds on love, devotion, attachment—it needs Michael to *choose* to stay, to keep loving the shell that was his wife. This anchors it permanently. The real Elena is still present, screaming from within. Michael realizes his love is feeding the thing killing her. **Chapter 6 – Desperate Measures** Michael seeks help—a professor specializing in folklore, a curandera. Learns of a separation ritual requiring the possessed to willingly sacrifice something of equal value to what the entity offers. Research intercut with the entity tightening its grip, punishing Elena for his resistance. Time is running out. **Chapter 7 – The Ritual** Michael performs the ritual. The entity fights, using Elena's voice, her memories, her love as weapons. Elena briefly breaks through, makes her choice—she will sacrifice herself to save him from the entity's permanent claim. The separation is violent, traumatic. The entity is torn from her, but the damage is catastrophic. **Chapter 8 – Aftermath** Elena survives but is profoundly changed—hollowed, fragile, missing pieces of herself. Their home feels foreign. Michael watches her sleep, hoping for the old Elena behind her eyes. She wakes, looks at him with love. It's her. But incomplete. They will learn to love each other again, differently. Ends with a moment of uncertain hope.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
## How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer would likely start with **emotional autobiography**—mining their own experiences of watching someone change, of feeling trapped between love and fear, of the particular intimacy of sharing a bed with someone who has become unknowable. They might think about a relationship that ended, a parent with dementia, a friend lost to addiction. The prompt's scenario—watching someone stare at you while you pretend to sleep—carries a specific texture of vulnerability and denial that a human would instinctively understand in their body. A human would probably **avoid symmetry and clarity**. Real relationships are messy. Real horror doesn't announce its rules. A human writer might not even know what the entity "wants" until they're deep in the draft, discovering alongside the narrator. They'd let the story be confusing in places, trusting the reader to sit with discomfort. Most importantly, a human would know that **the scarier thing isn't the monster—it's the grief**. Watching someone you love become unrecognizable is a universal human terror, whether through illness, addiction, mental health, or simply growing apart. A human would write toward that feeling, not toward the supernatural spectacle. --- ## Common LLM Tendencies & How They Might Fail This Piece **1. Over-explanation** LLMs tend to explain emotions, motives, and rules rather than letting them emerge. The entity "revealing its nature and purpose" in Chapter 5 is a classic example—a villain monologue that drains mystery. Horror needs the unknown to breathe. **2. Safe, Balanced Endings** "Hopeful but changed" is the LLM default resolution—not too bleak, not too neat. But this story is about profound violation and loss. A "bittersweet" ending might feel like it's pulling punches, refusing to sit in the darkness it created. **3. Structured Plot Over Emotional Logic** The ritual in Chapter 7 with "clear rules" transforms horror into a puzzle. Real possession stories work because they're about *helplessness*, not problem-solving. The professor/curandera in Chapter 6 is a trope—a convenient expert who provides answers. **4. Generic Relationship Details** "Establishing their loving marriage" often produces stock romance beats (meeting cute, shared jokes, meaningful glances) rather than the specific, sometimes uncomfortable texture of real partnership. **5. Pet Death as Emotional Shortcut** The cat being crushed is a common horror trope that signals "this is evil" in a manipulative, expected way. It's "safe horror"—shocking enough to register, but not genuinely disturbing in a personal way. **6. Heroic Victim** Elena "sacrificing herself" in Chapter 7 gives her agency and nobility. But possession is fundamentally about the *loss* of agency. Having her make a clean, heroic choice might feel like a comfort the story shouldn't offer. **7. Clean Structure** Eight chapters, each with a clear purpose, building toward resolution. Real horror fiction often has dead ends, false starts, and moments that don't "pay off." Too much structure can strangle dread. --- ## Deep Critique of the Current Plan **Chapter 1:** The plan to "establish their loving marriage" risks feeling generic. "Domestic moments feel almost-normal but wrong" is vague. Without *specific* details—who Elena actually is, what their relationship actually feels like—the dread won't land. The chapter might feel like it's checking boxes (here's the relationship, here's the first sign of wrongness) rather than immersing us in a lived reality. **Chapter 2:** "She speaks Spanish in her sleep" is a decent detail, but "unusual" suggests she doesn't normally do this—is she not a Spanish speaker? This needs clarification. The "flash of something cruel" is a bit on-the-nose. Horror works better with *wrongness that could almost be explained* before it becomes undeniable. **Chapter 3:** The cat death is my biggest concern here. It's a horror cliché that risks feeling manipulative rather than genuinely disturbing. It alsoexternalizes the horror (she kills a pet) when the story's terror should be more intimate (she's becoming something else in their bed, in their life). The "real Elena breaking through" moment could work, but "horrified" is an emotion we're told, not shown. **Chapter 4:** This is where the plan most clearly shows LLM thinking. A hidden journal, a village legend, an entity with a name and rules—this is *worldbuilding* rather than horror. The entity "speaking through her, mocking" is a villain monologue that removes all mystery. The scariest entities don't explain themselves. **Chapter 5:** An entire chapter dedicated to the entity "revealing its nature and purpose" and explaining what it "feeds on" strips away ambiguity. This transforms the story from "my wife is becoming something unknowable" to "here is the monster and here are its stats." Horror lives in the space where we don't understand. **Chapter 6:** The professor and curandera are convenient NPCs who exist to provide information. This is "protagonist goes to the library" structure. It also externalizes the solution—Michael doesn't have to grapple with impossible choices; he just needs to find the right ritual. The ticking clock ("time is running out") is a standard thriller trope that might not fit the slower, more intimate horror this story needs. **Chapter 7:** A "separation ritual" with a "willing sacrifice" is too clean. It transforms an intimate, devastating story into a magic system with rules. Elena "making her choice" restores agency to a character whose defining horror is the *loss* of agency. The "violent, traumatic" separation suggests spectacle over emotional truth. **Chapter 8:** "Hopeful but changed" and "they will learn to love each other again, differently" is a resolution that wraps up emotional threads too neatly. It's the "bittersweet but ultimately okay" ending that LLMs gravitate toward. But this story is about profound violation—the idea that things can be "different but okay" might feel like it betrays the horror that came before. The "moment of uncertain hope" is trying to have it both ways. --- ## Brainstorming: Making It More Human **For the Relationship:** - Specific rituals: The way she takes her coffee, the joke they've told a thousand times, her habit of falling asleep with one hand on his chest - Recent tensions: Not a "perfect marriage invaded by evil," but a real partnership with ordinary strains the entity exploits—maybe she wanted children and he didn't, maybe there was an affair, maybe they're just... tired - Physical intimacy: How the entity changes sex, touch, closeness—this is invasive in a way that violence sometimes isn't - The things he loved that are gone first: Her specific laugh, her competitiveness at board games, how she used to sing while cooking **For the Horror:** - **Less explanation.** The entity never monologues. Michael guesses, researches, fears—but never gets certainty. The grandmother connection can be suggested, not spelled out. - **Wrongness before violence.** She stops blinking normally. She stands in doorways for no reason. She hums a song he's never heard—but when he finds it, it's a lullaby from a Spanish village. - **The entity uses intimacy as a weapon.** Not just physical violence, but the violation of closeness—she knows his fears and uses them, she mimics intimacy without warmth. - **Elena's presence is chaotic.** When she breaks through, it's not "heroic victim"—she's confused, angry, maybe even blaming Michael. She wanted to go to Spain, he didn't stop her. Real victims aren't noble; they're desperate. - **Michael's denial is more realistic.** Not "he convinces himself it's nothing" for two chapters, but a genuine wrestling with the impossible. He looks for explanations. He questions his own sanity. He loves her and that love blinds him. **For the Plot Structure:** - **No convenient expert.** Michael finds fragments—online forums, a book, maybe one conversation with someone who fled the same village. But no one gives him answers. - **The ritual is dangerous and unclear.** Not a clear solution, but a desperate attempt based on half-understood folklore. It might not work. It might make things worse. - **Maybe no ritual at all.** Consider whether the story needs a "solution" or whether it's about living through something unfixable. **For the Ending:** - **Bleaker options:** Elena is gone. Michael has to choose between the shell that wears her face or losing her entirely. He chooses to let her go—but the entity doesn't leave cleanly. - **Messier options:** The "separation" works but Michael can no longer touch her without flinching. They're both damaged beyond repair. Love isn't enough. - **Ambiguous options:** He performs the ritual. She seems like herself. But in the final scene, she does something small—a gesture, a word—that makes him wonder if the entity is still there. No confirmation either way. - **Costlier options:** The entity can be banished, but it demands something in return. Michael loses something essential—his ability to love her, or trust anyone, or sleep without nightmares. The victory is pyrrhic. **For Voice and Style:** - **Let Michael be unlikable sometimes.** He's not the perfect husband heroically saving his wife. He's angry at her for changing. He feels guilty for wanting to leave. He has selfish thoughts he's ashamed of. - **Fragmentation.** As his mental state deteriorates, the prose can too—shorter sentences, repeated phrases, time jumps he doesn't remember making. - **Physical details over emotions.** Instead of "he felt afraid," describe his jaw aching from clenching, his hands shaking as he pours coffee, the way he checks the locks three times. - **Let scenes breathe.** Not every chapter needs a clear "purpose." Some can just be... living in the horror. Watching. Waiting. **Specific Scene Ideas:** - They're at dinner with friends and she laughs at the wrong moment, or says something that's *almost* right but slightly off, and he sees the friends notice - She starts humming while washing dishes—the same tune over over—and he realizes it's the song that was playing during her grandmother's funeral, which she couldn't have known - He wakes up at 3 AM and she's not in bed—he finds her standing in the dark, facing the corner, and when he touches her shoulder she turns and looks at him without recognition for a full three seconds before "returning" - She brings him coffee exactly how he likes it—but he'd never told her the specific way he takes it, and when he asks how she knew, she says "I've always known" with a smile that doesn't reach her eyes - A moment where he tries to have sex with her and she's... different. Wrong temperature. Wrong sounds. Wrong rhythm. And he can't finish because he's terrified. **Questions to Wrestle With:** - Does the entity need to "want" something, or can it simply *be*? - Is Elena still fully present inside, or is she being erased piece by piece? - Does Michael need to "win," or can the story be about losing? - What's the worst possible ending—not most shocking, but most emotionally honest?
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Plot:** Michael's wife Elena returns from her grandmother's funeral in rural Spain carrying something inside her. The entity has been slowly erasing Elena for months, and the staring is simply the first crack in its camouflage—it no longer needs to pretend as intensely. Michael watches the woman he loves become a stranger wearing her face, piecing together fragments of understanding without ever receiving clear answers. There is no expert, no clear ritual, no villain monologue. There is only a desperate husband trying to save his wife using half-understood folklore, and a wife who breaks through just often enough to ask him to let her go. The ending is not triumphant—the entity is banished at tremendous cost, and Elena survives but is fundamentally altered, missing essential pieces of herself. Michael stays, loving a woman who no longer remembers why she loved him back. **Voice:** First-person present tense. Confessional, exhausted, occasionally unreliable through denial rather than dishonesty. The prose should feel like a man thinking aloud in the dark—fragmentary when he's afraid, tender when he remembers who she was, clinical when he can't bear to feel. Physical details over named emotions. No grand philosophical statements. **Tone:** Slow-burn domestic dread. The horror is quiet and intimate—a spouse becoming unrecognizable across the breakfast table, in bed, in the small rituals of marriage. The supernatural is real but never fully explained or understood. Grief and terror are intertwined. **Aims:** Specificity in the relationship (inside jokes, physical habits, recent tensions). Ambiguity that doesn't resolve. A protagonist who is loving but also angry, exhausted, sometimes selfish. Wrongness that emerges from the familiar rather than the spectacular. An ending that sits in its own darkness without false comfort. **Avoids:** Explanatory villain monologues, convenient expert characters, clear "rules" for the entity, heroic sacrifice tropes, hopeful resolution, gore as substitute for dread, perfect victimhood for Elena. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – The Watching** Opens with the prompt. Michael lies still, breath controlled, while Elena stands at the bedside watching him in the dark. After she returns to bed, we see morning—their ordinary life together, their small rituals, a relationship that feels real and lived-in. He attributes her strangeness to grief over her grandmother's death and the stress of the trip to Spain. But when she makes coffee she's made a thousand times, she gets his ratio wrong—and when he corrects her, her confusion lasts a beat too long. He says nothing. He loves her. He tells himself it's nothing. **Chapter 2 – The Gaps** More staring episodes. Small moments of wrongness that he can almost explain: she forgets their anniversary is next month, misremembers how they met, laughs at the wrong moment during a movie they've seen together. She hums a melody he's never heard. He finds himself lying awake, watching her sleep—the reversal of the prompt—searching her face for the woman he married. She stirs, and for a moment her eyes open and there's no recognition at all. Then she smiles, drowsy and warm: "Can't sleep?" He's terrified. He says yes, he just can't sleep. **Chapter 3 – Recognition** At dinner with their closest friends, Elena tells a story about their wedding—but the details are wrong. Not just misremembered, but fundamentally off, as if she's describing someone else's life. Their friend Sarah notices, gives Michael a look. Later, Sarah pulls him aside: "Is she okay?" He doesn't know how to answer. That night, he tries to ask Elena what's happening. She tilts her head, studies him like a specimen, and says "I'm fine, Michael" in a voice that's almost hers but isn't. No anger, no monster reveal—just wrongness, quiet and complete. He realizes with cold certainty: she isn't grieving. She's disappearing. **Chapter 4 – The Grandmother** Michael searches Elena's belongings while she's at work. Finds her travel journal from Spain—not a clear exposition dump, but cryptic fragments about her grandmother's "sickness," about the old woman speaking to someone who wasn't there, about Elena feeling "watched from inside" after the funeral. A photo falls out: Elena's grandmother as a young woman, her eyes carrying the same flat stare Michael has been seeing for weeks. When Elena comes home, she catches him. Her face shifts into something cold and amused. Then, suddenly, she gasps, her hand flies to her mouth, and it's *Elena*—terrified, present, begging him to help her before she's pulled under again. The entity resurfaces, calm: "She can't hear you." **Chapter 5 – Pretending** Michael doesn't know what to do. He can't sleep. He can't leave her. He Googles obsessively, finds fragments—village forums in Spanish, scattered folklore, a mention of an entity passed through bloodlines that feeds on proximity and attachment. Nothing clear. He has to go to work, smile at colleagues, pretend his life isn't collapsing. Elena goes through the motions of being his wife—cooking, touching him, talking—but he sees the mimicry now. She touches him in bed, and he flinches. The entity notices. Studies him. Adjusts. Tries again, more convincingly. He realizes it's learning. He has to keep pretending, or it might stop trying to hide. **Chapter 6 – The Fragment** Michael finds a potential solution—half-remembered folklore about a separation ritual, requiring salt, fire, and the possessed's true name. It's vague, possibly useless, possibly dangerous. He considers leaving, saving himself. Then Elena breaks through again—this time in the middle of the night, shaking him awake, whispering: "It's getting stronger. You have to do something. Or let me go." She doesn't tell him what to choose. She's not a heroine making a sacrifice. She's exhausted, fragmented, begging. He holds her until the entity returns. He decides to try the ritual. **Chapter 7 – The Burning** Michael attempts the ritual. It's crude and desperate—salt in a circle, candles, Elena's grandmother's name, Elena's name, words he's pieced together from conflicting sources. He expects nothing. But then Elena begins to seize, to scream, and he hears *two voices*—one the entity, ancient and furious, one Elena's, crying out in terror. The entity tries to bargain, threaten, mimic his wife perfectly. He keeps going, sobbing, not knowing if he's killing her or saving her. At the peak, Elena's eyes find his—*her* eyes—and she says, "Michael, I'm sorry. Let me go." He makes a choice. He completes the ritual. Silence. **Chapter 8 – After** Elena survives. She's alive. But she's diminished—quiet, hollowed, missing six years of memories including the entirety of their marriage. She knows facts (she lives here, this is her husband), but she doesn't *feel* them. She looks at Michael sometimes with mild confusion, sometimes with something that might be fondness. He stays. He learns who she is now. In the final scene, he watches her sleep—a deliberate echo of the prompt—searching her face for something familiar. She stirs. Opens her eyes. Stares at him for a long moment, expression unreadable. Then she smiles, uncertain, sleepy: "Can't sleep?" He doesn't know if the entity is truly gone or just quiet. He'll never know. He gets back into bed beside her, and he closes his eyes.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Michael **Background & Personality** Michael is 34, a technical writer for a healthcare software company—a job he tolerates rather than enjoys, one that exercises his patience for translating complexity into plain language. He's good at it. He's good at explaining things. This makes what's happening to Elena unbearable, because it cannot be explained. He grew up in Ohio, the younger of two sons in a family where emotions were handled quietly and privately. His father left when Michael was sixteen, and Michael's response was to become the "easy one"—the child who didn't make trouble, who handled things, who didn't ask for more than what was given. This shaped him into someone who avoids conflict, who swallows his needs, who tells himself he's fine when he isn't. He loves Elena in a way that surprises him—a steady, unstudied love that has become the foundation of his adult life. But he's also grown complacent. He lets her carry the emotional weight of their relationship: she plans the dates, remembers his mother's birthday, notices when the car needs an oil change. He's always been content to let her lead. Now that she's disappearing, he's forced to confront how much he relied on her, and how little he gave back. **Flaws:** - Uses humor and detachment to avoid difficult conversations - Passive in relationships, expects love to maintain itself - Has a streak of selfishness he doesn't acknowledge—he doesn't want to lose her, but he also doesn't want to be alone - Prone to rationalizing, explaining away warning signs to avoid confronting the truth - Carries guilt about the relationship that he's never voiced: she wanted children, he kept saying "maybe next year" **Idiosyncrasies:** - Listens to history podcasts to fall asleep; the droning voices quiet his mind - Cannot cook beyond scrambled eggs and grilled cheese; Elena did all the real cooking - Has a small scar on his chin from a childhood bike accident he's embellished into a better story - Drinks coffee black but doesn't actually like it; started in college to seem tougher - Picks at the cuticle of his left thumb when anxious, sometimes until it bleeds - Keeps a messy desk but organizes his books by color **Physicality** Tall but not imposing—six feet with poor posture that makes him seem smaller. He has the look of someone who was athletic in college and has since softened; he talks about running but his running shoes gather dust by the door. His face is pleasant, forgettable, with a jawline that's begun to blur. Dark hair he cuts too infrequently, usually pushed back haphazardly. He carries tension in his shoulders and jaw. He fidgets without realizing—tapping fingers, bouncing a knee, touching his wedding ring when he's nervous. His default expression is politely neutral, practiced from years of work meetings. He rarely cries; the last time was at his wedding, and even then he was embarrassed by it. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** His primary motivation is to get his wife back—but tangled beneath that is guilt, fear of abandonment, and a desperate need to fix something he's only now realizing he may have neglected. He loves Elena, but he's also terrified of being alone. He's never had to be an adult without her, and the prospect of that emptiness is paralyzing. He has a brother in Portland he speaks to twice a year. His mother is alive but emotionally distant; they send cards on holidays. Elena became his family, his home, his entire social world. Her friends became his friends. Without her, he has no one. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue** *Internal monologue:* > "This is what love looks like, I told myself. This is what it does. It makes you crazy. It makes you lie awake at two in the morning watching someone sleep and wondering if you ever really knew them at all, or if you just fell in love with a version of them that you invented, and now the real person is finally showing up, and she's nothing like the one you married." *Dialogue:* > "I don't know how to explain it. She's... off. Like a recording played at the wrong speed. You know when someone's face doesn't match their voice? It's like that. Like that all the time now." --- # Elena **Background & Personality** Elena Vega is 32, a pediatric nurse at a county hospital—a job she loves fiercely, the kind of person who remembers every child's name, brings in Halloween decorations for the ward, and cries in her car after a difficult shift. She is, in Michael's words, "the one who feels things for both of us." She was raised in Arizona by her mother, a second-generation Mexican-American who worked double shifts as a hotel housekeeper. Her father wasn't in the picture. Her grandmother—her mother's mother—lived in a small village in northern Spain, in a house that had been in the family for generations. Elena visited once as a teenager, a summer she never talked about much. There were photos. In all of them, she looked away from the camera. She met Michael at a friend's birthday party seven years ago—he was the only one who didn't make fun of her when she accidentally knocked over a table of drinks. She loved him for that. She loved him for the way he listened, the way he made her laugh, the way he never asked her to be smaller than she was. She loved him even when he couldn't give her the things she wanted—children, a house with a yard, a husband who planned anniversaries. She told herself she could wait. **Flaws:** - Carries old wounds she refuses to examine; deflects with humor or busy-ness - Tends to mother everyone, including Michael, sometimes to her own exhaustion - Fears being alone more than she admits; stayed in the relationship even when her needs weren't met - Can be stubborn, refuses help, insists she's "fine" when she isn't - Avoids conflict to the point of self-erasure **Idiosyncrasies:** - Hums while cooking—always the same half-remembered melody, something from childhood she can't place - Cannot sleep without one foot sticking out from under the covers - Writes in the margins of books, argues with the author in pen - Cries at commercials featuring dogs or elderly couples - Keeps a "favorite words" list in her phone: *petrichor, luminescence, nevertheless, dulcet* - Steals the blankets and has zero remorse about it - Laughs loudly and sometimes snorts; embarrassed by it but Michael loves it **Physicality** She is small—five-foot-three—and moves like someone larger, taking up space without apology. Dark wavy hair she usually wears in a messy knot, escaping in tendrils by noon. Her face is animated, mobile; you can always tell what she's thinking. Freckles across her nose she covers with makeup for work. A small scar on her collarbone from a childhood bike accident—she and Michael have matching ones now, a coincidence she called fate. Her hands are her most expressive feature. She talks with them, touches people when she speaks, reaches out without thinking. When she's sad, she crosses her arms tight. When she's happy, she bounces slightly on her heels. **What the Possession Takes:** First, the small things: the way she used to hum while cooking, her competitive streak during game nights. Then the bigger pieces: her warmth, her expressiveness, her ability to look at Michael and truly see him. Her face becomes still in moments it shouldn't be. Her hands stop reaching out. **After the Ritual:** She survives, but diminished. She remembers facts—she's a nurse, she lives in this apartment, she's married to this man—but the emotional weight is gone. Six years are simply not there. She looks at Michael and sees a stranger she's supposed to love. Sometimes she feels something, a pull, a shadow of what was there. Mostly she feels confused, hollowed, exhausted. She stays because she doesn't know where else to go. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Before possession, Elena wanted what many people want: to be loved fully, to build a family, to find meaning in her work and her relationships. She wanted Michael to want the same things, and when he didn't, she folded that want away somewhere dark and tried not to look at it. She loved her grandmother, but from a distance. She flew to Spain when the old woman was dying, sat at her bedside, held a hand that felt too cold. She doesn't remember much of that final night—just the feeling of being watched, of something pressing against the inside of her skin. **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue** *Dialogue (before):* > "You're doing the thing again. The face. The 'I'm listening but actually thinking about work' face. I can see it, Michael. I can always see it." *Dialogue (during, the entity speaking):* > "You keep looking for her. She's not coming back. She's very far away now. She asked me to tell you she's sorry, but I don't think she is. I think she's tired." *Dialogue (after, a fragment of the real Elena breaking through):* > "Michael. Michael, I can feel it. It's wearing my face. I can feel it wearing my face. Please, please, you have to—" *Internal monologue (Michael's memory of her voice):* > "You want to know what love is? It's stupid. It's the dumbest thing. It's staying up late to talk about nothing. It's remembering how someone takes their coffee. It's all the small things, Michael. It's only ever the small things." --- # The Entity **Nature & Origin** It has no name, or if it does, no human tongue could pronounce it. It is old—not ancient in the way that monuments are ancient, but ancient in the way that mountains are, in the way that the dark spaces between stars are. It has been passed through Elena's maternal line for at least four generations, possibly longer. Her great-grandmother called it "the weight." Her grandmother called it "the sickness." Elena didn't know to call it anything at all. It does not think in human terms. It has no gender, no true form, no comprehensible desires. It is, in some essential way, a parasite—but not a biological one. It feeds on connection. Attachment. The invisible threads that bind people to each other. It is most sustained by love, the most concentrated form of human bond, and it has learned to inhabit those bonds, to curl inside them and drink. **How It Works** It enters when the host is vulnerable—during grief, during trauma, during moments when the self thins. Elena's grandmother died; Elena flew to a foreign country, sat at a dying woman's bedside, and grieved in a language she half-knew. The entity had been waiting in the grandmother for decades, patient and dormant. When the old woman died, it transferred. It does not destroy the host immediately. It needs the host's connections, relationships, the web of attachment. It needs the people who love the host to keep loving. So it learns. It studies. It mimics. It practices Elena's expressions in the mirror at three in the morning. It memorizes her laugh, her gestures, her small cruelties and larger kindnesses. It gets better over time. The staring is its natural state. When it stops pretending, it watches. Studies. Observes the humans the way a scientist might observe an experiment, or a farmer might observe livestock. **What It Wants** It wants to continue. To feed. To be anchored in the world through the host's connections. Michael's love for Elena is sustenance—the stronger his attachment, the more he gives, the more it can take. It does not want to kill him. It wants him to stay. **How It Manifests** - **The stare:** Flat, still, unblinking. No human looks at another human that way. - **The coldness:** A physical chill emanating from Elena's body, especially her hands. - **The mimicry:** Her laugh is almost right but not quite. Her gestures are learned, copied, slightly off in timing. - **The gaps:** It cannot access Elena's memories perfectly. It forgets things she would never forget. It misremembers. It makes small mistakes. - **The voice:** When it speaks, the voice is Elena's but flattened—like a recording with the warmth engineered out. **Flavor Dialogue** *Spoken through Elena:* > "You're afraid. I can taste it. It tastes like copper. I don't understand why you're still here. Most of them leave by now. Most of them stop loving. But you—you keep holding on. Why do you keep holding on?" > "She's not suffering. She's not anything. She's just... not here anymore. I'm here now. And I'm very, very hungry." > "You should have let her go. She wanted you to let her go. But you wouldn't. You never could." *What it might say if it were honest (but it never is):* > "I don't hate you. I don't feel anything for you at all. You're food. You're a wire in a circuit. I need you to love her so I can exist. That's all. That's the only reason I haven't killed you."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping. The room was dark except for the faint glow of the streetlight through the curtains, and she stood at the foot of the bed, motionless. Her face was angled toward me, eyes open, fixed. She wasn't blinking. I kept my breathing slow and even, my eyes just barely slitted, watching her watch me through my lashes. The digital clock on my nightstand read 3:17. She stood there for what felt like an hour but was probably only minutes. Her body had the stillness of a photograph, chest barely moving. Finally, she turned, walked around to her side of the bed, and lay down with careful precision. The sheets rustled. Then nothing. I waited until I heard her breathing shift into the rhythm of sleep—or something like it—before I let myself exhale. In the morning, she made coffee. This was our ritual. Elena ground the beans while I showered, and by the time I emerged, damp and fumbling for pants, there'd be two mugs waiting on the counter—hers with a splash of cream, mine black. She knew exactly how I liked it. She'd known for six years. "You want eggs?" she asked, already pulling the carton from the fridge. "Sure." I leaned against the counter, watching her. She moved differently in the morning now—slower, more deliberate. I'd told myself it was jet lag, then grief, then just the general exhaustion of returning to work after two weeks away. Her grandmother's funeral had been hard. Harder than she'd expected, she'd said. The old woman had been distant, senile near the end, but Elena had loved her in that complicated way you love family you don't entirely know. She cracked the eggs one-handed, a trick she'd learned in nursing school, and they hissed in the pan. The kitchen smelled like butter and coffee and the faint, lingering ghost of her perfume. I sipped mine and frowned. It was bitter. Too bitter. I pulled back and looked at the mug, then at her. "Something wrong?" She didn't turn from the stove. "No, just—" I stopped. The coffee was fine. It was probably fine. I was being paranoid. "Nothing. Just woke up on the wrong side, I guess." She turned then, spatula in hand, and smiled. It was a good smile. It looked like her smile. "You always say that when you don't sleep well." "Did I say something last night? Talk in my sleep?" "No." She tilted her head slightly, considering. "Why?" "Just wondering." I took another sip, forced myself to swallow. "Bad dreams." She went back to the eggs. I watched the line of her shoulder, the familiar curve of her neck, the way her hair fell forward when she leaned over the pan. I wanted to touch her. I wanted to cross the kitchen and wrap my arms around her waist and press my face into that spot between her shoulder blades where she always smelled like herself. But I didn't. I hadn't, not since she'd come back from Spain. Not because she'd pushed me away, exactly—just because something had felt off, like a door left slightly ajar, and I couldn't bring myself to step through it. We'd met seven years ago at a birthday party for a friend of a friend, the kind of crowded apartment gathering where you don't know most of the people and you end up talking to whoever's standing nearest the drinks. She'd knocked over a table of beer bottles, and everyone had laughed except me. I'd helped her clean up, and she'd looked at me with something like surprise, like she'd expected mockery and gotten something she didn't know what to do with. She still looked at me sometimes, in that same way. But not lately. "I'm taking the car today," I said. "Meeting across town." "Okay." She slid the eggs onto plates, handed me mine. Her fingers brushed mine, and they were cold. Cold the way hands get when you've been holding something frozen, except she'd been standing over a hot stove. "Elena." She looked up. Her eyes were dark, steady. They didn't waver. "Are you okay?" I asked. "I mean, really okay. You've been... different. Since you got back." Something flickered across her face. A pause, like a skipped frame in a video, like a lag between the expression and the feeling behind it. "I'm fine," she said. "Just tired. It was a long trip." "I know. I just—" I set my fork down. "I worry about you." Her expression softened. She reached across the counter and touched my hand, deliberately, like she'd remembered she was supposed to. "I know you do. I'm okay. I promise." I nodded. I ate my eggs. I went to work. But the coffee had been wrong. She'd been making my coffee the same way for six years—two scoops, no more, no less. She'd measured it by heart for so long that she didn't even need to think about it. And this morning, she'd made it too strong. By half a scoop, maybe. Just enough to notice. It was nothing. It was absolutely nothing. But on the drive to work, I couldn't stop thinking about the way she'd stood at the foot of the bed at 3:17 in the morning, staring at me in the dark. The way her eyes had looked flat, depthless. The way she'd turned and walked back to bed with such precise, careful steps, like she was learning how to move in a body she'd just acquired. I shook my head. I turned up the radio. I told myself I was being ridiculous, that grief did strange things to people, that she'd been through a lot and I was supposed to be the supportive husband, not the paranoid one. I told myself she'd get better. She'd go back to normal. We'd laugh about this in a few months—remember when you were so worried about nothing? But I couldn't shake it. The image of her standing there in the dark. Not moving. Not blinking. Just watching.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This opening chapter demonstrates strong craft in establishing domestic horror through subtle wrongness rather than overt supernatural elements. The writing successfully creates an atmosphere of creeping dread by focusing on small deviations from normalcy - the too-strong coffee, cold hands, precise movements - that feel genuinely unsettling without being melodramatic. The first-person present tense narration works effectively, creating immediacy and intimacy that draws the reader into Michael's growing paranoia. The voice feels authentic - a man trying to rationalize away his fears while being unable to shake his unease. The prose is clean and controlled, avoiding overwrought language while maintaining tension. Character work is particularly strong. Michael comes across as genuinely human - loving but passive, observant but prone to self-doubt. His internal conflict between wanting to support his grieving wife and trusting his instincts feels real. Elena, even in her possessed state, maintains enough recognizable humanity to make the wrongness feel like a violation rather than a replacement. The chapter follows its plan faithfully, opening with the prompt and establishing the domestic setting, their relationship history, and the first clear signs that something is amiss. The coffee detail is perfectly chosen - specific enough to feel significant, small enough that Michael questions his own perception. However, some elements feel slightly predictable for the horror genre. The 3:17 AM timing, the standing-at-the-foot-of-the-bed image, and the "cold hands" detail are familiar horror tropes, though they're executed competently. The backstory exposition about how they met, while character-building, feels somewhat inserted rather than organic to the scene. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves character development well, though it doesn't particularly stand out. The emotional engagement is solid - we care about Michael's confusion and fear, and the relationship feels lived-in enough that we understand what he stands to lose. The chapter succeeds in its primary goal of establishing unease while maintaining plausible deniability about whether something supernatural is actually occurring. This ambiguity is crucial to the story's effectiveness and is well-maintained throughout. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 It happened again three nights later. I woke at 2:44 to find her standing in the doorway of the bedroom, backlit by the hall light she never left on. She was facing me, hands at her sides, perfectly still. When I shifted on the mattress, she didn't react. Her eyes were open—I could see the glint of them—but she didn't seem to be looking at anything. Just looking. "Elena?" I said, my voice thick. She blinked, once, and something in her posture changed. Her shoulders relaxed. Her head tilted slightly to the left, the way it always did when I caught her doing something she didn't want me to see. "Sorry," she whispered. "Didn't mean to wake you. Bathroom." She turned and disappeared down the hall. I heard the door close, the click of the lock. I stared at the ceiling for a long time before I fell back asleep. In the morning, the hall light was off. I didn't ask her about it. --- The first time I noticed the humming, we were eating dinner. Nothing special—she'd made pasta, the way she always did on Tuesdays, with that jarred marinara she pretended was homemade. I was halfway through my second glass of wine when I realized she'd been doing it for several minutes. A low, tuneless melody, the same five notes over and over. Hummed through closed lips, almost a moan. Beautiful in a strange, uncomfortable way. I stopped eating. "What is that?" She looked up from her plate, fork paused halfway to her mouth. "What?" "That song. What is it?" Her expression flickered—confusion, then something else. Something closed. "I don't know. Just something in my head." "It's pretty." "Is it?" She went back to eating, and the humming stopped. I didn't hear it again for two days. Then, on Friday morning, I woke to the sound of her in the shower, that same five-note pattern drifting through the bathroom door. When she came out, towel wrapped around her hair, I asked if it was an old song her mother used to sing, maybe something from childhood. She looked at me like I'd asked her the capital of Mars. "I don't remember," she said. --- I started watching her differently. Not obviously—I didn't want her to feel examined—but quietly, in the spaces between normal interactions. The way she moved through a room. The way she held her coffee cup. The way she responded when I said something funny. She laughed at the wrong moment during a movie we'd seen twice before. It was a drama, a tense scene where the protagonist discovers her husband has been lying to her. Elena laughed—loud, sudden, harsh. Then stopped, abruptly, when she realized I was staring at her. "Sorry," she said. "I thought—he looked funny. The way his face—" She didn't finish the sentence. She just shook her head and went back to watching. --- Our anniversary was next month. Six years. I knew this because I'd been quietly researching restaurants, trying to decide between the Italian place she loved or the new seafood spot downtown. I wanted to do something special. I wanted to give her something she'd been asking for—a weekend away, maybe, just the two of us. I'd been distant lately. Preoccupied. I wanted to make up for it. "June twelfth," I said one evening, testing. We were on the couch, her feet in my lap, some reality competition playing on the TV. "What's June twelfth?" She didn't look away from the screen. "Hmm?" "June twelfth. What's that date?" "Your mom's birthday?" I stared at the side of her face. "No. That's October." "Right. Right, sorry." She glanced at me, smiled in a distracted way. "What is it, then?" "Our anniversary." Something moved behind her eyes—recognition, or a reasonable imitation of it. "Of course. Our anniversary. Six years." She squeezed my knee. "I didn't forget. I just—my brain's been scattered lately. Work. You know." I knew. She'd been pulling extra shifts at the hospital, covering for a colleague on maternity leave. At least, that's what she'd told me. --- The worst part—the part I didn't want to admit—was that I'd started lying awake after she fell asleep, watching her. The reversal of those first nights, when she'd been the one standing in the dark. Now I was the one staring, searching her face in the moonlight for something familiar. The woman I'd married. The woman who laughed too loud at stupid jokes, who cried at commercials with dogs, who hummed while she cooked because she couldn't stand silence. She looked the same. Same dark hair spread across the pillow. Same small hands curled near her face. Same faint scar on her collarbone, the one she'd gotten falling off her bike when she was seven. Same body I'd memorized over six years of mornings and nights and lazy Sunday afternoons. But there was something missing. Some quality of stillness that hadn't been there before. She slept like a photograph now. Barely moved. Didn't twitch or murmur or reach for me in the night. One evening, I was watching her sleep when her eyes opened. It was sudden. No slow drift toward consciousness—just a single instant where her eyes went from closed to open, fixed on the ceiling. Then they shifted to me, and there was nothing in them. No warmth, no recognition, no love. Just a flat, measured calm. I stopped breathing. A moment passed. Then her expression softened, and she smiled—sleepy and warm and entirely familiar. "Can't sleep?" she murmured. I swallowed. My heart was pounding so hard I could feel it in my throat. "Yeah," I managed. "I just—yeah. Can't sleep." She reached out and touched my arm. Her fingers were warm. "Come here." I let her pull me close. I pressed my face into her hair and breathed in the smell of her shampoo, her skin, her. I told myself it was fine. I told myself I was being crazy. I told myself she was right there, solid and real, and I was inventing monsters in the dark because I couldn't handle a little distance, a little grief, a wife who'd come back from her grandmother's funeral slightly different than she'd left. But I couldn't stop thinking about the way her eyes had looked in that moment before she smiled. Like she'd never seen me before in her life.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter effectively builds on the established premise with careful attention to psychological realism and mounting dread. The writing demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the intimate, domestic horror tone established in the plan. The characterization of Michael is particularly well-executed. His growing awareness manifests through specific, believable observations rather than dramatic revelations. The reversal where he becomes the watcher is psychologically astute and creates effective dramatic irony. His internal conflict between love and fear feels authentic, as does his tendency to rationalize disturbing behavior. The portrayal of Elena's possession is subtle and effective. Rather than obvious supernatural manifestations, the wrongness emerges through small details: laughing at inappropriate moments, forgetting their anniversary, the strange humming. The entity's imperfect mimicry creates genuine unease without resorting to cheap scares. The prose style matches the established voice well - confessional, exhausted, with physical details prioritized over emotional exposition. Sentences like "She slept like a photograph now" effectively convey wrongness through concrete imagery rather than abstract description. The chapter structure builds tension methodically through escalating incidents. Each scene adds another layer of wrongness while maintaining plausible deniability, which serves both Michael's psychology and the reader's growing unease. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The dialogue occasionally feels slightly stilted, particularly Elena's responses which sometimes read as overly careful rather than naturally evasive.
